item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this. Some of the dialogue was a bit cheesy, which kind of took me out of it, but the atmosphere and overall mystery surrounding the article sold it for me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fun story +1

Little nitpick: in the 12th experiment log the following phrase occurs: “ All though it seems that these experiments ultimately failed, ”

Pretty sure that should start with “although”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I liked it. despite some cliches, it was very enjoyable to read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I largely agree with AlanDaris's view that all of the potential horror that comes from a powerful organization utilizing their funds to extract the souls of children and place them inside of arcade machines are boiled down to cheap scares and artificially creepy children chanting in a monotone voice1. The final addendum is painfully cliché, with the child giving all three of the most mind-numbing descriptions of a potentially anomalous murderer imaginable: an angel, a dark man, and a liar. It simply kills all sense of uniqueness and charm that this article had left, which was what was keeping it afloat for me.

A smaller note, but I don't like the inclusion of the fading text on the first addendum. While it works in an article like SCP-5656, which is a heavy format screw, it doesn't add anything here besides just shortening the one section where I could potentially empathize with the parents of these missing children. Thus, it again kills a lot of the potential emotional investment that I could've shared with these characters.

This is a -1 from me.

Footnotes
1. This is definitely not helped by the needless inclusion of Satanic rituals or the ability of the children to somehow infect other parts of the restaurant as referenced in the second Event Log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, first of all, I'm a big fan of creepy stories centered around kids restaurants and mascots (who are terrifying on their own), and I was interested to see what this piece offers. Well, there is some atmosphere and aesthetic here, but outside of that, the piece didn't hit me that much.

This feels like it has several cool ideas, but they're boiled down to tired cliches from horror literature and aren't explored any further. First of all, we are told immediately what the reason for the machines malfunctioning is, it's the missing children who supposedly died and got their souls transferred there (or something). This detail removes any mystery from the big chunk of the piece and makes it seem far less engaging than it could've been. It's pretty pointless to show us various hints and details about the children being there if you've already told us everything at the beginning.

The twist with Arcadia using children to produce an advanced AI is… Fine I suppose, but not that special considering the concept is hardly new or original. Outside of that, I don't see a realistic goal of this project: the problems caused by missing children and people finding a connection to Arcadia far outweigh the possibility of making some arcade machines better. Also, the fact that the Foundation was hurting the kids via playing seems to be thrown in there haphazardly to invoke more emotions, but it's not that big of a twist (again, because we know what's up with the arcane machines pretty much from the start) and isn't explored much further beyond the cryptic messages, which lose their effectiveness due to being shown so late into the article. Similar topics were done right before, take SCP-4361 for instance, in which the Foundation researcher realizes how it hurt the anomaly and how horribly she feels because of this.

I understand what the piece attempted to do, but due to its structure, it ended up feeling pretty uninteresting for the most part.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
I got that, yeah, but it still kinda feels off in a way

I dunno, overall I'd say this is pretty good

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: While playing the game, each loss hurts the children inside.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… yeah, I still dunno

Stuff like that never particularly grabbed me - objectively speaking, the Foundation didn't do anything to them, so I'm not sure why they would accuse the Foundation of causing their pain

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: I can see that. I'll change that up

Overall I liked it, but

Response: it brought us here. but it was you who made us.

This was kind of cringey?

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Image Creds
Don't sue Jak or Kara plz

The "Arcade" image utilized in this article has been licensed under CC SA-BY 3.0. It has been provided by Coenter of Wikimedia Commons. No substantial edits have been made, only minor cropping of the background. Original link: https://commons.m.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Tilt_byte_-_1.jpeg#mw-jump-to-license

The "Instance" image utilized in Addendum 5963.2 was created by user JackalRelated, who has given the authors of this piece, JakdragonX and KaraKatt, explicit permission to use it. If you wish to contact the original creator, please utilize Wikidot's PM to contact any of the aforementioned users. Logs can be given on request.

Creds to Critters
Thanks Everyone!

fish_men_hate_jelly
Its a Bad Idea
JackalRelated
ItsDenali
Cerastes
Doctor Saffron

I'd like to take a moment and thank SCPD for giving me this absurd and ridiculous concept. As you can probably tell, this was inspired by several aspects of Scott Cawthons Five Nights At Freddy's series, and something I'm personally quite happy with.

I'd like to also thank KaraKatt for dedicating the time and effort into making this possible. Without Kara this might not have ever happened. They put in a crazy amount of work into it, so I'm glad I could help them out with their first SCP!

Other than that? I'm surprised this thing is still floating.
